title: Lentil and bacon soup
subtitle: Smoky, thick, and satisfying
excerpt: Red lentils simmered with smoked bacon lardons, carrots, and celery into a thick, hearty soup. One of the most inexpensive and filling meals in the repertoire. Ready in 40 minutes.
sourceNotes: A standard of British home cooking, widely published in post-war cookery books. The combination of smoked bacon and lentils draws on French and British traditions of using cured pork to flavour pulses.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool and freeze in portions for up to three months. Thickens considerably on freezing. Thin with hot water or stock when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. A useful batch-cook soup to freeze in weekday portions.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves over two days in the fridge as the lentils continue to thicken and the flavours deepen.

glossaryTerms.definition: Small cubes or strips of cured, often smoked, pork belly or back bacon. Used to add a smoky, fatty flavour base to soups, stews, and salads.

content.text: Lentil and bacon soup is one of the most dependable weekday meals: cheap, filling, and better after a night in the fridge. The red lentils dissolve into the broth without any soaking or blending, which means the only thing that needs attention is the initial frying of the
and vegetables. Everything else takes care of itself.
Heat a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add the {{lardons}} of smoked lardons and fry for 5 minutes until golden and the fat has rendered. Spoon out a few tablespoons to garnish later.

Add the {{onion}} onion, {{carrot}} carrots, and {{celery}} celery to the pan. Cook in the bacon fat for 8 minutes until softened. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic, {{thyme-dried}} of dried thyme, and {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée. Cook for 1 minute.
Add {{lentils-red}} of rinsed red lentils and pour over {{stock-chicken}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook for 25 minutes until the lentils have dissolved into the soup.
Taste and season. The bacon provides a lot of salt; add cautiously.
Ladle into bowls. Scatter the reserved crispy lardons over the top and add a handful of flat-leaf parsley if using.

items.cause: Red lentils thicken considerably as they dissolve.
items.fix: Add hot water or stock 100 ml at a time and stir. Red lentil soups routinely need thinning on reheating.

items.cause: Smoked lardons are salty, and some stocks are too.
items.fix: Use low-salt or homemade stock. Taste before seasoning and add salt only at the very end.

items.symptom: Lentils are still grainy after 25 minutes
items.cause: Very old lentils, or the simmer was too gentle.
items.fix: Buy lentils from a shop with high turnover. Increase the simmer slightly and cook for another 10 minutes. Older lentils can take up to 40 minutes.

items.symptom: Bacon is not crispy enough as a garnish
items.cause: Too much fat in the pan or bacon was added before the pan was hot.
items.fix: Fry the reserved lardons separately in a dry non-stick pan over a high heat for 3 to 4 minutes until properly crisp before adding to the bowl.

content.text: Smoked pork with pulses is a combination found from the French cassoulet tradition through to British pea soup with ham hock. Lentil and bacon soup sits in the middle of that tradition: not as formal as a French lentilles du Puy or as long-cooked as a ham hock soup, but a reliable weekday version that most households have a version of. The red lentil, which dissolves without soaking or blending, makes it one of the easiest thick soups to cook.
